label: delphi-r1-p2
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the leading cases and how courts have actually applied the rule to messy facts.
text: {"probability":80,"confidence":"high","position_summary":"A court applying City of San Jose v. Superior Court would very likely hold that the Signal messages — discussions by sitting councilmembers of franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will realistically accept,\" and RFP timing — meet the content-based definition of \"public records\" under Gov. Code § 6252(e) regardless of their location on personal devices/accounts, and would very likely find the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer deficient because the city never inquired of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications before answering, a search-adequacy failure San Jose's own remand contemplated. The proposition is well-supported but not certain, mainly because the auto-deletion of most messages complicates how definitively a court can characterize the destroyed content versus the ~40 preserved screenshots.","rationales":["San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes public-records status turn on whether content 'relates to the conduct of the public's business,' not on the account or device where it is stored, and the stipulated subject matter here (franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing) sits at the core of that test rather than its margins.","Because three sitting councilmembers were direct participants discussing a matter pending before their own body, this presents the strong-content-nexus scenario San Jose treated as clearly covered, unlike the harder edge cases (e.g., low-level staff or third-party communications) the Court left more open.","San Jose's remand instructions and the CPRA duty-to-assist framework (Gov. Code § 6253.1) require agencies to make a reasonably calculated inquiry into likely repositories of records, including asking individual officials to check personal accounts when there is reason to believe public business was discussed there; searching only 'city systems' without any such inquiry falls short of that standard as a matter of law.","The ~40 surviving screenshots alone are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed outside city systems at the time of the request, making the 'no responsive records' answer factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the deleted messages.","Some residual uncertainty exists because a court may distinguish between declaring the surviving screenshots public records (clear) and formally characterizing the auto-deleted, unrecoverable messages as having 'been' public records (murkier, resting on witness recollection and inference rather than the writings themselves), which could lead a careful court to hedge the scope of its holding even while finding a search-adequacy violation."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 6252(e) (definition of 'public records')","Gov. Code § 6253.1 (agency duty to assist requesters)","Gov. Code § 34090 (records retention and destruction)","Consider verifying whether any post-San Jose trial-court or appellate decisions on remand or in analogous cases have specifically addressed search-adequacy standards for personal-device messaging apps used by elected officials"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will extend a formal 'these were public records' holding to the bulk of auto-deleted messages that no longer exist and can only be characterized through witness recollection and the 40 surviving screenshots, versus limiting its clear holding to the preserved screenshots and treating the rest as a retention-schedule/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA public-records determination."}

data.position_summary: A court applying City of San Jose v. Superior Court would very likely hold that the Signal messages — discussions by sitting councilmembers of franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and RFP timing — meet the content-based definition of "public records" under Gov. Code § 6252(e) regardless of their location on personal devices/accounts, and would very likely find the clerk's "no responsive records" answer deficient because the city never inquired of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications before answering, a search-adequacy failure San Jose's own remand contemplated. The proposition is well-supported but not certain, mainly because the auto-deletion of most messages complicates how definitively a court can characterize the destroyed content versus the ~40 preserved screenshots.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes public-records status turn on whether content 'relates to the conduct of the public's business,' not on the account or device where it is stored, and the stipulated subject matter here (franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing) sits at the core of that test rather than its margins.

Because three sitting councilmembers were direct participants discussing a matter pending before their own body, this presents the strong-content-nexus scenario San Jose treated as clearly covered, unlike the harder edge cases (e.g., low-level staff or third-party communications) the Court left more open.

San Jose's remand instructions and the CPRA duty-to-assist framework (Gov. Code § 6253.1) require agencies to make a reasonably calculated inquiry into likely repositories of records, including asking individual officials to check personal accounts when there is reason to believe public business was discussed there; searching only 'city systems' without any such inquiry falls short of that standard as a matter of law.

The ~40 surviving screenshots alone are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed outside city systems at the time of the request, making the 'no responsive records' answer factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the deleted messages.

Some residual uncertainty exists because a court may distinguish between declaring the surviving screenshots public records (clear) and formally characterizing the auto-deleted, unrecoverable messages as having 'been' public records (murkier, resting on witness recollection and inference rather than the writings themselves), which could lead a careful court to hedge the scope of its holding even while finding a search-adequacy violation.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 6253.1 (agency duty to assist requesters)

Consider verifying whether any post-San Jose trial-court or appellate decisions on remand or in analogous cases have specifically addressed search-adequacy standards for personal-device messaging apps used by elected officials
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will extend a formal 'these were public records' holding to the bulk of auto-deleted messages that no longer exist and can only be characterized through witness recollection and the 40 surviving screenshots, versus limiting its clear holding to the preserved screenshots and treating the rest as a retention-schedule/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA public-records determination.
